title: Azure Logic Apps - Common Scenarios, Patterns, And Operational Branches
applies_to: Routing Logic Apps requests by scenario and solution pattern
Choosing the right Logic Apps branch for triggers, control flow, deployment, monitoring, and enterprise integration

confirmed_facts.fact: The examples and scenarios guide positions Azure Logic Apps as a serverless integration platform for orchestrating actions across multiple systems and services by using more than 1,400 prebuilt connectors.

The guide organizes Logic Apps work into common starting points, migrations, control flow, code execution, security, reliability, Foundry integration, content transformation, custom APIs and connectors, B2B, virtual-network access, deployment, management, monitoring, and end-to-end scenarios.
The page distinguishes polling triggers, which check an endpoint on a schedule, from push triggers, which wait for an external event and fire when the event happens.

Common polling trigger examples on the page include Recurrence, email-received triggers, and HTTP-based checks of a service endpoint, while common push trigger examples include Request and HTTP Webhook.
The guide explicitly includes migration paths from BizTalk Server and from Power Automate to Azure Logic Apps Standard.
The page groups advanced workflow logic under control-flow and error-handling capabilities such as conditions, switches, loops, scopes, and exception handling.
Logic Apps code-execution examples on the page include JavaScript snippets, Azure Functions, and in Standard workflows .NET code, C# scripts, and PowerShell scripts.

The guide includes dedicated branches for securing workflows, reliability, Foundry tools and AI capabilities, content-type handling and transformations, custom APIs or connectors, B2B integration accounts, virtual-network access, deployment, management, and monitoring.
For direct access to secured resources in an Azure virtual network, the page points to creating a Standard logic app workflow in single-tenant Azure Logic Apps.

The deployment branch on the page includes DevOps and ARM-template automation, and the monitoring branch includes workflow monitoring, run-status review, alerts, diagnostic collection, Defender for Cloud monitoring, and Application Insights for Standard workflows.

scenario_selection_rules: Use the common-starting-points branch first when the question is mainly about trigger style, workflow bootstrap, or whether the workflow starts on a schedule or on an event.
Use the control-flow branch when the problem is conditional logic, loops, scopes, or exception handling rather than connector choice.
Use the code-execution branch when the workflow needs inline code, Azure Functions, or Standard-only script execution instead of only designer actions.

Use the custom-API-or-connector branch when the target system is not covered by a connector and the solution needs HTTP plus Swagger, API Management, App Service, or custom connector design decisions.
Use the security and reliability branches when the request is about identity, tenant restrictions, resilience, zone redundancy, or disaster recovery.
Use the Foundry and AI branch when the request involves document parsing, LLMs, chunking, or AI-enriched workflows.
Use the B2B branch when the request is about AS2, X12, EDIFACT, RosettaNet, trading partners, or integration accounts.
Use the VNet-access branch when the workflow must reach private Azure resources directly; this is a Standard-oriented design question.
Use the deploy-manage-monitor branches when the user asks for productionization, CI/CD, cost management, logging, telemetry, or operational ownership.
implementation_notes: Treat this page as a scenario router for Logic Apps, not just an examples page. It maps user requests to the correct capability family quickly.

When planning a Logic Apps solution, first classify the request into one of these branches: trigger pattern, control flow, code execution, security, reliability, AI, B2B, private networking, deployment, or monitoring.

If a request spans several branches, keep the design modular. For example, an enterprise workflow can require trigger design, managed identity, private networking, error handling, and monitoring all at once.
Use this entry to decide which more-specific KB entries to load before building or documenting a Logic Apps solution.
caveats: This page is a hub of scenario links, not a substitute for the deeper how-to pages it references.
Many branches differ materially between Consumption and Standard, so scenario selection must still be paired with hosting-model selection.
Some scenario links point to tutorials, samples, blogs, GitHub assets, or whitepapers; use official Microsoft Learn pages as the primary authority for reusable implementation facts.
